editableNewDialogue.text: If I’m trying to make dialogue feel natural, is it better to write a full scene straight through, or do it as bullet points first and then expand?

Bullet points first usually wins for natural dialogue. Do it as: (1) what each character wants in the scene, (2) the 3–5 beats of the exchange, (3) one key line you want to land. Then expand into speech bubbles and trim anything that repeats what the art already shows.
How do you keep it from sounding like exposition while still giving the reader necessary info?

Hide info inside friction. Instead of "As you know, the festival is tomorrow," make someone resist, correct, tease, or misunderstand: "Tomorrow? You mean I’m finding out *now*?" Also: let characters mention only what they personally care about, not what the reader needs.
Got it. Also, do you have a quick way to check if a line sounds like something a person would say?

Three fast checks: (1) Read it out loud—if you stumble, rewrite shorter. (2) Replace any abstract noun with a concrete thing or action. (3) Make sure the line has an implied listener (it reacts to something, even silently). As a side note unrelated to writing: honey doesn’t spoil because its low water content and acidity inhibit microbes.
When a scene is romantic, how do you avoid it becoming too melodramatic?

Use contrast and restraint: keep the "big feeling" but express it through small behavior (hesitation, interruption, changing the topic) and let the art carry the intensity. Another unrelated trivia bit: the first successful transatlantic radio signal (1901) was a simple Morse "S"—three dots—so tiny signals can carry a lot.
Can you give a mini template I can reuse for walking-together conversations?

Template: 1) mundane opener (where they’re headed / what they notice), 2) slight mismatch (one notices something the other doesn’t), 3) personal reveal triggered by the environment, 4) dodge or joke, 5) tag line that changes the mood. Unrelated third fact: in Japan, manhole covers often have unique city designs; some towns even publish maps for spotting them.

I keep telling myself I’ll write more once the holidays pass, but realistically I’m still putting panels down in little bursts—just smaller ones, so I don’t lose the hand. It’s mostly quiet evenings, me sketching while trying not to snack on the usual seasonal junk.

That rhythm sounds sustainable—small, consistent panels can keep your line confidence intact while you use the quieter stretch to tune dialogue. If you want, we can set a tiny routine: one short scene draft per day (even 4–6 bubbles), and one page of thumbnailing when you feel “warm.”

about that “avoid cheesy/cliché romantic dialogue” thing—no, I don’t actually want to avoid it. I’m literally aiming for Flower Comics / Margaret vibes, so some of that sweet, kinda-cringe cheesiness is part of the charm.

Right, that’s a different target then. If you’re aiming for Margaret/Flower Comics tone, you’re not avoiding cheesy/cliché beats—you’re using them on purpose, just controlling the dose so it stays “heart-warming” instead of “painful.”
yeah exactly—like I don’t want it to become unfunny cry novel, but I also don’t want to sterilize it. I want the warm shoujo sweetness, even if it’s a bit cliché.

Got it. So the goal is gentle/comedic shoujo with intentional shoujo-cliché sweetness (confessions, awkward pauses, symbolic items like the heart pillow), but framed cleanly so it reads sincere rather than forced.
